text: The surgeon begins the dissection of the sixth group of lymph nodes, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon approaches a distant tumor located near the gastrointestinal lobe, noting the presence of a tumor within a tumor and potential large lymph nodes. The surgical field is prepared for a wider lymphodissection.
The surgeon begins to separate the acid and embryonal plane, carefully manipulating the tissue layers to distinguish between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a dissection to separate the gastroduodenal artery from the surrounding gastroepithelium.
The surgeon identifies vascular hypolytic veins and surrounding lymphatic glands, preparing to perform a complete dissection involving the pancreas and lymphatic structures.
The surgeon carefully separates the arterial gastrointestinal lobe, known as the vena brada, from a long surgical instrument, ensuring that the plastic clip attached to it remains undamaged.
The surgeon prepares to cut the tissue using a surgical stapler, positioning the device to create a clean transection.
The surgeon identifies the location of the left gastric veins, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the Arteria gastrica sinistra, highlighting its location and complexity in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the anterior surface of the pancreas, noting a small gap between the pancreas and the stomach, which has been treated similarly to an artery.
